metadata.title: The Hard Case
metadata.subtitle: Differentiation That Preserves Cognitive Demand
metadata.source_chapter: Agent Skills for K-12 Teachers (Anthropic) — k12-lesson-differentiation
metadata.description_blurb: Most differentiation strips challenge from struggling students. This video shows the cognitive-demand principle: same hard case, different door in. Three tiers on 17 ÷ 5 — concrete array, symbolic expression, abstract proof — all arrive at the remainder together.
metadata.reformat: center-cut, focus-aware; pantry/<bid>-916.* is the human replacement slot; -916 overrides honored

beats.narration_text: Hola, I'm Liam — in for Bear. Every differentiation strategy has a hidden trap. When you make the task easier for the students who are struggling, you quietly rob them of the very thinking you're trying to build. Today we look at a better principle: same hard case, different door in.

props.command: I have a class reading at three very different levels. What's the most important thing NOT to do when I differentiate the lesson?

beats.narration_text: Seventeen divided by five. Three tiers — all solving the exact same problem. Below tier students use a concrete array: draw seventeen dots, group them by five, circle the two left over. At tier: the symbolic expression, three remainder two, with a basket analogy. Above tier: the same equation, then a proof challenge — why must the remainder always be less than the divisor? The scan across all three tiers reveals the point: the hard case, the remainder, lives in every tier. No one was protected from the thinking.

props.sparkLine: Same hard case. Different door in.

beats.narration_text: The principle generalises. Differentiation adjusts the scaffold, the representation, the entry point — never the intellectual demand. When you give the struggling reader a simpler book, you solve today's problem and create next month's gap. When you give them the same text with more support, you give them a chance to close it. The scan arrow in that figure is the tell: all tiers converge on the same conceptual hard case.

artifactLines: Scaffold density (how much structure is provided)
The hard case (the remainder — the conceptual moment)
Better scaffold → same hard task, more support.

beats.narration_text: Here's the test for whether differentiation has become tracking. Ask one question: does every tier arrive at the same intellectual destination? If the below-grade tier only practices with simpler tasks at a lower ceiling, you haven't differentiated — you've sorted. Universal Design for Learning calls this the ceiling check. Widening the path means multiple doors into the same room. Forking the path means some students never reach the room at all.

props.sparkLine: Differentiation widens the path. Tracking forks it.

beats.narration_text: Cognitive load theory gives us the partition. Extraneous load is waste — confusing formats, redundant instructions, unnecessary procedural steps. Remove it. But germane load — the actual intellectual struggle with the concept — that is the curriculum itself. When we simplify a task by removing the challenge, we've committed a category error. We moved the learning from the germane pile to the removed pile. The scaffold should absorb the extraneous friction. The learner must keep the germane.

props.sparkLine: The scaffold absorbs extraneous. The learner keeps the germane.

beats.narration_text: Claude can build a tiered version of almost any math or reading task inside a single prompt. The principle to hand it: preserve the hard case, vary the representation. Ask it to show you three tiers side-by-side so you can verify the cognitive demand is the same before it goes to students.

props.artifactHeading: Differentiation changes the door, not the destination.
artifactLines: Below: Draw 17 dots in groups of 5. Circle the 2 left over. Name the remainder.
At: 17 ÷ 5 = 3 R2. Explain with baskets — why are 2 apples left?
Above: Prove why the remainder must always be less than the divisor.
All tiers arrive at the same conceptual hard case: the remainder.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Take a lesson you already have. Tell Claude the main concept and the hard case — the moment where students most often get stuck. Ask it to design three tiers using concrete, representational, and abstract representations, keeping the hard case in all three. Then compare the tiers: does each one actually arrive at the same intellectual demand? That comparison is the whole skill.

props.command: Here is my lesson objective and the hard case where students struggle most. Design three tiers — concrete, representational, abstract — that all arrive at the same conceptual moment. Show me all three side-by-side so I can verify the demand is preserved.
